title: ExpressPath: RNA-seq Time Course Analysis Protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"db26h","text":"ExpressPath is a pipeline that takes your raw RNA-seq count data and","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1admc","text":"produces a complete differential expression analysis with time course and","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c2qvs","text":"cross-cell-line comparisons. It runs from a single command and gives you:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"epi7f","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3ooio","text":"  - Lists of genes that respond to treatment (and when)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"u8o1","text":"  - Classification of genes into temporal patterns (transient, sustained, etc.)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9ogij","text":"  - Pathway enrichment (which biological processes are affected)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fu626","text":"  - Transcription factor analysis (which regulators drive the response)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cddvb","text":"  - An interactive HTML report you can open in any browser","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"87lpu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a8i34","text":"GitHub: https://github.com/bruPav/ExpressPath","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":8,"length":37}],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"F4529C76688611F199BB0A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://github.com/bruPav/ExpressPath"}}}}

Type \"y\" if asked to confirm.)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1eusp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2t0jf","text":"    Verify: snakemake --version","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9duvn","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4islr","text":"Input data:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"644v5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"j86l","text":"A tab-separated file (.tsv) with gene expression counts.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6mhnp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1j75q","text":"Required columns:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dbbg2","text":"    - First column:  Gene ID (e.g., ENSG00000000003). No header needed.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"713d1","text":"    - gene_name:     Human-readable gene symbol (e.g., TSPAN6).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6ofrc","text":"    - Count columns: One per sample. Any column names work.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eolkl","text":"    - Annotation columns (GO terms, KEGG pathways, protein families, etc.)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e3lv7","text":"      are carried through to output if present (common with eggNOG-mapper).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"72jf5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dg54l","text":"Example TSV:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2bmha","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e1oll","text":"        ,gene_name,A549_mock_1,A549_mock_2,A549_1h_1,...","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dq7v1","text":"        ENSG00000000003,TSPAN6,150,162,145,...","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dvr38","text":"        ENSG00000000005,TNMD,0,0,0,...","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cg35o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2cbi","text":"How to get this file:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bkfon","text":"    - featureCounts output is already in this format.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9sgcg","text":"    - STAR + RSEM: use rsem-generate-data-matrix.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bp7ll","text":"    - Salmon/Kallisto: use tximport in R.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"be0f6","text":"    - If in doubt: rows = genes, columns = samples, values = integer counts.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"86B4462D657611F192290A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://docs.conda.io/en/latest/miniconda.html"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"86B44690657611F192290A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh"}}}}
steps: Open your terminal (Terminal on macOS/Linux, or WSL on Windows) and run: git clone https://github.com/bruPav/ExpressPath.git cd ExpressPath This downloads the pipeline into a folder called "ExpressPath" and moves you into it. You should see files like "run.sh", "README.md", and folders named "pipeline/", "data/".

Copy your count TSV file into the "data" folder inside ExpressPath: cp /path/to/your/counts.tsv data/ Replace "/path/to/your/counts.tsv" with the actual path to your file. The filename can be anything (e.g., "my_experiment_counts.tsv"). Just remember this name - you will need it in the next step.

The experiment design tells the pipeline: - What your cell lines are called - What your time points are - Which TSV column belongs to which sample - Which comparisons to run Two options — choose one: OPTION A — Use the browser-based setup tool a) Open the file "pipeline/setup_design.html" in your browser. - Double-click it in your file manager, or - Right-click -> "Open with" -> your browser, OR - In terminal: xdg-open pipeline/setup_design.html b) A form appears. Fill in: Experiment Info: - TSV File: Click "Browse" and select your counts TSV - Treatment Name: e.g., Ad26 - Time Points: click "+" to add: mock, 1h, 3h (The first time point is the reference/baseline.) Cell Lines: - Click "Add Cell Line" for each one. - Enter the name (e.g., A549). - Set the number of replicates. - For each replicate, pick the matching column from your TSV. - (Optional) Set a batch label if samples were processed separately. Comparison Flags: Leave all four switched ON (default). You can turn off specific types later by editing "data/design.yaml" by hand. c) Click "Download design.yaml". Save it into the "data/" folder inside ExpressPath. Make sure the filename is exactly "design.yaml". OPTION B — Edit the template by hand Copy the example and open it in any text editor: cp data/design.example.yaml data/design.yaml nano data/design.yaml (or use gedit, vim, Notepad, etc.) Edit the following: - source_tsv: "your_counts.tsv" - Under "cell_lines": change "A549" and "E6" to your lines - Under "timepoints": change list to yours - Under "column_map": replace each sample column name with yours from the TSV. Format: "YourSampleColumnName": {cell_line: "A549", time: "mock", replicate: 1, batch: "1"} Save and close.

If needed, edit "pipeline/config.yaml" in a text editor: nano pipeline/config.yaml Settings you might change: pvalue_threshold: 0.05 <- significance cutoff count_filter_threshold: 10 <- minimum reads to keep a gene temporal_n_clusters: 6 <- number of expression patterns gsea_min_set_size: 15 <- smallest pathway to test The defaults work well for most experiments. You do NOT need to change anything for your first run.

In the terminal, make sure you are inside the ExpressPath folder. Then run: ./run.sh What happens: - The script checks that conda, snakemake, and your design are ready. - If design.yaml is missing, it offers to help you create one. - Snakemake starts processing: the first run installs R packages automatically (this takes 10-20 minutes and only happens once). - You will see progress messages as each analysis step completes. Options: ./run.sh -n #Dry-run — shows what would run without running it. ./run.sh -j8 #Use 8 CPU cores (faster; adjust to your machine). ./run.sh -n Dry-run — shows what would run without running it. ./run.sh -j8 Use 8 CPU cores (faster; adjust to your machine). Runtime: ~15-30 minutes for a typical 2x3x3 design on a modern laptop. The first run is slower because conda installs packages.

When the pipeline finishes, the terminal prints the path to your report. It looks something like: Report: results/20260611_143052/pathway/interactive_report.html Output: results/20260611_143052/ The timestamp (20260611_143052) is generated automatically from the date and time of the run. Open the report: - Double-click the HTML file in your file manager, or - Type: xdg-open results/20260611_143052/pathway/interactive_report.html Expected runtime: First run: 30-60 minutes (installs R packages automatically) Later runs: 15-30 minutes (packages already installed) Disk space: output is typically 50-200 MB. Re-running: each run creates a new timestamped folder in results/. Old results are never overwritten. Batch effects: if your samples were processed in different batches, assign batch labels in the experiment design (Step 3). The pipeline includes batch as a covariate in the DESeq2 model. Use the PCA batch plot (qc/pca_batch_plot.pdf) to verify correction.
creator: Bruno Pavletić
